channel.title: Posts on DAEBFCG
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on DAEBFCG

item.title: Installing Electronics
item.description:
The components were placed on the mdf template and the solder connections made. The wiring was done if four phases.

  1. Shield/Grounding (green wire)
  2. Signal connections (yellow wire)
  3. Signal return ground (black wire)
  4. Capacitors (black wire)

It is quite a rats-nest once it is all together.

A couple last-minute changes from the schematic:

  1. I skipped adding the resistor to the volume pot to drop it to 333k, and just left it at the 484k it is.

1.Instead of the “blocking capacitor” I found something that seems a bit better, this guy which uses a 220k resistor and a .022 cap in parallel for shock prevention. It is even mentioned in a Dan Erlewine book that I have, so it must be legit.

Grounding the Tokoy Cosmos pots and the Carling switches

To create a solid connection to the shielding ground (the user-touchable components) I added some homemade thin copper lugs to the bushings of the pots and switches that get wired to a single lug that is screwed into the side of the guitar into the shielding. This lug is then attached to the signal ground at the jack via the aforementioned safety-connector.

So in theory touching the metal pot shafts or metal knobs, and metal switch actuators will not be directly connected to the signal ground, but instead connected to the shielding ground which is effectively grounded but has the cap/resistor in between for safety (and possibly noise isolation).

The Tocos pots are a bit unusual in that the backs of the cases are metal but are not electrically connected to the bushings and shaft. Usually these have continuity so by grounding one you ground the other. I did not feel like soldering a wire between these points for fear of damaging these pots, so used a bit of the copper shielding tape to make a connection between the back casings and the bushings. This might be overkill, since the guitar cavity is fully shielded and the pot backs probably will not be picking up interference.

Installation

Once the circuit is all wired up, I had to get it into the guitar cavity. The was a horrendous process but it worked in the end.

And it is in!

Amazingly it all worked as expected.

The only slight glitch was the pickup selector toggle. For some reason, in the bridge position the leaves 1 and 2 were not making solid contact, and getting some neck pickup bleed through. Not sure what happened, but I managed to fix it by pushing the leaf and bending it back into place for a tighter connection.

Wiring Done

Got all the knobs back on and now to play it for a while.

item.title: Shellac
item.description:
My finish of choice for luthiery is shellac. It is completely natural and non toxic and enhances the chatoyance of wood like no other finish. It is extremely thin so lets the wood vibrate and does not dampen the resonance of the instrument (unless excessive coats are applied.)

It comes in flake form.

I make small, 1 ounce batches. To make a “one pound cut” mixture I use 1/8 ounce of shellac and 1 ounce of spirit (alcohol).

The best spirit to use is Everclear 190 proof grain alcohol.

I do not drink it but I heard you can.

These are combined in a jar and left tightly sealed up overnight for the flake to dissolve.

The freshly mixed up shellac can be used the next day. It lasts a month or two, but since I use small batches I usually use it up. Any leftover goes into another jar that can be used for sealing timbers.

An entire instrument takes about two batches (2 oz total).

item.title: Finishing the Guitar
item.description:
This is a minimalist French Polishing.

The instrument was previously sanded to 1000 grit and has one seal-coat of 1-pound-cut shellac. I use 1-pound cut for everything.

This seal coat stays on for the initial string up and test-playing stage. When it is time for the final finish to be applied, the process goes like this:

  1. Lightly sand to 1000 grit (dry). Any trouble spots or noticed scratches are sanded with 800-400 grit if needed, and down to 1000 again.

  2. Apply around seven light coats of shellac in continuous succession. Sometimes called a “bodying” session. Rub out with alcohol.

  3. Cure for around 72 hours.

  4. Wet sand with 1000 grit, using walnut oil as the wetting/de-clogging medium. Clean off all oil and dust with naphtha (Zippo fluid).

  5. Apply another 5 or so coats of shellac, and rub out with alcohol again.

  6. Cure for 72 hours.

  7. Wet sand to 3000 grit with walnut oil and clean off with naphtha.

  8. Apply one light top coat of shellac.

  9. Cure for 72 - 96 hours before handling.

Stand

I devised a simple way to hold the guitar while finishing. This takes advantage of two places that do not need to be finished and will have hardware covering them in the end: the tuner holes and the holes for the bridge/string-through ferrules. The posts have little rubber bolt-caps on them, which work well for preventing slipping but are soft enough not to mar.

Tools

The one tool for shellacing is the muñeca, aka “rubber”, aka pad. This is made by tightly wrapping a wad of cheesecloth in a square of old cotton t-shirt. Drops of shellac are applied to the muñeca using a pipette to “charge” it.

A small dropper bottle of spirit (Everclear) is used for the final rub out of a bodying session.

Second session

Starting to get glossy.

Curing

Between sessions, the shellac needs to cure for a few days. I typically use a mic stand, but this solid body guitar is too heavy so I hung it on my door.

Reassembly

Once the final top coat has cured, the hardware can be put back on.

item.title: Making Knobs
item.description:
First successful attempt at making some wood knobs.

The key to knobs that fit and stay secure on pots are these brass set-screw inserts.

I started by gluing up four, one-inch blocks of maple. Did not get a picture of that, but after it was turned on the lathe it looked like this:

These were sawn apart into their respective blanks and disc-sanded to a consistent height. These are 5/8 inch tall.

Why not just make them from a long dowel? Because I want the tops to be side grain, not end grain.

Next the pilot holes are drilled in the bottom center, and the holes in the sides for the set screws. The set screw holes are 7/64 inch (and are 5/16 up from the bottom).

The skirting recess is drilled out of the bottom first, using a 3/4 inch forstener bit. This goes in about 1/8 deep. The purpose of this is so that the knobs can sit flush on the guitar face, but cover over the washer and nut of the pots.

Not shown, but finally the hole for the insert is drilled. This is a 3/8 wide hole.

Once the inserts are tapped in the knob can be installed on a dowel and put in the drill press for sanding.

The finish is ONE COAT of Tried-and-True Danish oil.

They look decent and work well on the Super K. Much better than the cheap plastic speed knobs.

These knobs are rather basic. I would like to try adding numbers somehow, or maybe a “pointer” of contrasting wood, and maybe shaping them a little more than just simple cylinder. Super K II will get the next iteration of knobs when the times comes.

item.title: Control Placement Finalized
item.description:
Template

The template for drilling the holes was made in Libre Office Draw by taking a photo of the original control cavity cutout template, sizing to 100%, and then arranging models of the components to fit. The component models were made to their actual size to ensure everything will fit within the boundary of the cavity without touching or overlapping each other.

Once I got the layout determined, I printed out the template and did a test run on a sheet of mdf. The mdf sheet was cut into the shape of the control cavity to double-check that none of the components would be going past the edges. This also gave me a chance to check the functionality of the layout and make sure things were not too close together and each switch and knob could be easily reached and actuated.

This took me four tries to arrive at an arrangement that I was happy with.

Drilling the holes

One challenge was making sure that the template was positioned correctly on the top the guitar in relation to the inside cavity. Fortunately I saved the original template cutout of the cavity and used it as a guide to draw light pencil lines on the face of the guitar representing the cavity. Positioning this took some careful measuring and estimating, and finally verified by using a rare earth magnet on the side with a small screw on the top to ensure the lines were actually in the correct place.

I then drilled 3/64 pilot holes, and drilled to their final size using forstener bits. 3/8 inch for pots, 1/2 inch for the switches.

I made sure to get clean holes without any tear out by starting the hole from the inside and then finishing from the front.

A Major Screw Up

A stupid and easily avoidable mistake

While drilling from the inside, I accidentally went all the way through on one hole and ended up blowing out wood around the hole in the top.

I should have pre-drilled from the top first, then the back, and finally all the way through the top. That would have prevented this error.

I need to fix this.

Test Installation

The controls in place. Looking good.

A bit tight on the inside. Getting this circuit wired and soldered up and back in is going to be fun.

Fixing a Hole

A few options for dealing with the blow-out around the hole

  1. Sand it out and deal with the resulting indentation
  2. Sand the entire front of guitar down past the depth of the missing chunk.
  3. Glue the missing chunk back in and sand smooth
  4. Patch in some filler shavings and sand smooth

Did not want #1. #2 seems unfeasible considering the depth of the gouge. I would like to try #3, although I think I lost the chunk of wood that got blown out, unfortunately. I have some chunks that are close, so will try that.

This patch chunk was hide-glued in and clamped down. I may have had better results using some shavings as they might confirm the the gouge better.

Once the glued cured I chiseled away the paper and extra wood.

Then sanded smooth.

Of course it does not look perfect but at least the top surface is flat with no gouged out area. I think it will look like variations in the wood grain and will not be very noticeable. The nut that screws down over the switch bushing might cover this up a little, too.

Finally, this half of the guitar top was sanded down to bare wood again, and re-sealed with shellac.

Crisis averted. You can see it if you look for it, but nobody will see if from six feet away. Best case scenario is that I forget all about this.

Next the final stages of finishing can be completed.

item.title: Circuit and Components
item.description:
Final Version

Updated schematic:

A few changes from before.

  • Volume pot RV4 is specified as a 333KΩ, This is a reduction from the original 500K. It sounds slightly smoother and less harsh. Lots of single-coil pickup guitars use 250K for this reason. There is a slight drop in overall volume but it is almost imperceptible. I thought 250 was a bit too soft sounding, so added a 1MΩ resistor across the 500K pot to make it 333 for a good middle ground.

  • Treble tone capacitor is now .022 µF instead of .015 µF. Just slightly more treble and mid roll off.

  • Treble and Middle tone share the same capacitor C1. Since they are both now .022 µF, they can utilize the same capacitor, which is an old Fender trick. In their case, they are saving a couple pennies on a component and also avoiding doubling up the capacitance when two tone controls are used together in parallel. In my case, the combining is not an issue but it does save on a capacitor.

  • Addition of a .33 µF blocking capacitor C4. This is a safety measure which I learned about from guitarnuts. All user-touching components (strings, bridge, knobs, etc) and the shielding are connected to ground as usual but separated from the signal ground by this capacitor. The purpose is to prevent reduce operator injury if amp voltage ever gets sent back to the guitar over the ground wire path. Not sure if this is really necessary but they make a good case so I figured why not.

Components

I have finally accumulated all the necessary components. I tried to get the most durable, highest-quality parts.

C1 .022 µF Polystyrene Capacitor. This is a new-old-stock MIAL polystyrene, made in Germany probably in the late 1970s. There are rare to find this big. I really like polystyrene. They sound better than anything else in my experience. Some people like paper-in-oil, some folks like orange drops, and some people say the type does not matter at all and only the value of the capacitance affects the sound. And they make a great logical case for this argument. At any rate, these polystyrenes look cool and there is some good counter argument that they are superior for audio. They are relatively cheap too, compared to other popular types. (More info here) My meter says this one is 22.5 nF.

C2 1000 pF silver mica capacitor for the bass tone control.

C3 Pair of 750 pF silver micas to be used in parallel for the required .0015 µF in the “Fuzz” tone. I could not find a nice looking .0015 µF cap for this, so two half-size together works just as well.

I did not bother measuring these silver micas. They sound good.

C4 .33 µF, 630V blocking cap. Just a run of the mill polyester metalized film.

J1 Puretone jack

Also shown is the Electrosocket tele-style jack plate.

SW1, SW2, SW5 Carling SPDT On/On switches. These are full size (not huge, but not minis either) and very durable. I decided to skip using any mini switches and went with these heavy duty guys. I want this guitar to be rock solid and not need wiring repairs too soon in its lifetime.

I was also worried these large switches would add some extra resistance but they only measure at .2 Ω which is virtually nothing. I checked some cheap mini switches I had and they were in the .4 to .5 Ω range, so I guess these are higher quality.

Capacitance wise, they are showing .2 nF, although my meter is not super accurate at these low levels. Mini switches look like around .2 to .3 nF as well. So I am inferring that these Carling switches will add equal or less unwanted influence on signal quality compared to mini switches.

RV1 Tokyo Cosmos, 1 MΩ Audio-Taper pot, for the mid tone. These “TOCOS” are rather high end and cost $10 each. I have not used them yet but they feel smooth and should sound good and not crap out in the near future. Checked on my meter, this one actually measures at a resistance of .894 MΩ.

RV2 Tokyo Cosmos, 500 KΩ Audio-Taper pot, for the treble tone. Actual measured resistance: 472 KΩ.

RV3 Tokyo Cosmos, 1 MΩ Audio-Taper, for the bass tone. Actual measured resistance: .955 MΩ

RV4 Tokyo Cosmos, 500 KΩ Linear-Taper, for the volume. I find linear taper for volume is more usable than audio taper, which many other people agree with although audio seems to be the more conventionally used taper for volume controls. To me, the linear volume change is more continuous and does not have the quick spike / drop-off in the “8-10” range that I hear with audio taper pots. Actual measured resistance: 486 KΩ.

SW3 AxLabs 6-Lug, 2-Pole toggle switch. Decent quality toggle switch that can be wired as on-off-on. And in black! The superior alternative might be the Switchcraft model like this that is used for double-necks, but is too expensive for now. This AxLabs switch when engaged is measuring at .35 nF capacitance and .5 Ω resistance. Probably negligible but technically inferior to other switches? Interesting…

SW4 Carling DPDT On/On for the “Firehose” (pickups direct to jack) switch. Measurements same as the SPDTs.

Knobs

All black, solid aluminum knobs here. The 1/2 inch diameter are for the tone controls. This is smaller than usual, but I think still usable and fits the aesthetic. The volume is 3/4 inch, which is a bit more normal size.

I wonder if the lack of numbers will bother me. I do like to get a sound dialed in and being able to get it back by turning to a specific number. We will see.

The real issue is how to fit all this into the guitar.

item.title: Pickup Mounting
item.description:
My first attempt at mounting the pickups utilized #3 screws (humbucker mounting screws) directly into the wood. Machine screws do not hold firmly in wood, especially this small, so this did not work very well. I had planned to inset nuts into the top of the holes for the screws to grab, but insetting nuts this small and flush was not feasible.

The updated solution uses these brass threaded inserts with #4 screws.

The inserts are screwed into 5/16 diameter holes in the floor of the pickup routes.

These provide a solid receptacle to the pickup mounting screws. I am using small lengths of rubber tubing for the “springs” to push the pickups up against the screw pressure, providing height adjustment, and the keep them from bottoming out and crushing the wires.

Getting these inserts positioned precisely is a tedious job. But overall I like this solution. The hold is very solid with no risk of anything coming loose or wearing out.

item.title: New Tone Control Option
item.description:
This introduces another switch for two useful and distinct treble-tone options. The added option bypasses the typical treble-tone signal flow and connects the signal directly to ground through a small capacitor.

This sort of mod is often used on Telecasters. I gave it a try and found it highly effective for a mid-rangey, cutting, almost fuzzy, searing lead tone.

On this guitar, I found the .0015 µF capacitor worked best, which is coincidentally one order of magnitude smaller than the cap I like for the normal tone.

1. signal → 500K Pot → .015 µF capacitor → ground.
2. signal → .0015 µF capacitor → ground.

Alternatively, the switch could be placed after the pot and have it function as a capacitor-selector only, which is often a nice option. This does add the ability to dial in more or less of the effect of the small cap. However the small cap is mostly noticeable when the pot is on 0 (no resistance to ground). So I find it more useful to be able to alternate between the small cap with no resistance and the normal cap with higher/variable resistance just by flipping the switch for a more dramatic change.

Updated Schematic

Added the “TrebleCap” switch and additional capacitor.

Example Recordings

Pickups in series (bridge full, neck tapped), mids 0, bass 10

Original .015 µF cap, treble 10:

New .0015 µF cap direct to ground:

Bridge pickup only (full), bass on 10

Original .015 µF cap, treble 4:

New .0015 µF cap:

A few more for comparison

.015 µF cap

Pickups in series (bridge full, neck tapped), mids 0, bass 10, treble 5:

Pickups in series (bridge full, neck tapped), mids 0, bass 10, treble 3:

.0015 µF cap

Pickups in series (bridge full, neck full), mids 10, bass 0:

item.title: Drawing Schematics
item.description:
My first schematic, made for the guitar circuit.

KiCad Schematic Editor

I am just recently getting comfortable reading and creating schematics. I found a nice oss editor that is part of the KiCad suite of tools.

This program seems good although there is some learning curve. It is helpful to know the keyboard commands for the various tools. The most useful is “A” to place an item. You hit A and are presented with a modal that lets you pick from thousands of various schematic symbols.

To draw a wire between components, you enable the Wire tool by hitting W. It is best to have the cursor ON the pin where you want the wire to start. This is because you do not click down to draw like a typical drawing program, but instead it just starts making a wire as you move the mouse starting with where you were when the tool was enabled.

Escape

Important Tip: Any time you want to get back to the main Select tool, just hit ESC.

Deleting

One annoying aspect is that anytime you click a wire it automatically puts you into Wire tool mode, and is is easy to draw bunch of unwanted wires. Deleting short little wires is harder than it seems like it should be.

Export

Oddly, it does not export to an image file, but instead image data to the clipboard. Not a big deal, just and extra step to create image for use in another program.

item.title: Circuit Test Box
item.description:
New circuit testing rig made from a cigar box.

The inside is fully shielded.

Some mini breadboards for making and connecting modules. The rear-left one is intended to be the main output, and is connected to the output jack in the back. It also has the kill-switch wired to it which comes in handy while arranging connections.

I installed a little handle to lift up the inner lid. I removed it later realizing I can just use one of the attached knobs.

The output jack:

The components are attached from the inside and their wires go up through the holes to be connected to breadboards. I could use some more holes. I made some holes 1/4 inch for the mini switches and some 3/8 for the pots and rotary switches. I probably should have made them all 3/8. Also could use some 1/2 inch holes. Always easier to add more holes and make existing ones bigger.

This also serves as a storage compartment.

It is meant to be able to be closed up, for storage or even more shielding. One design flaw here is that it cannot be fully closed with wires and components sticking up without mashing them down.

My circuit

This is much neater and easier to deal with than the previous modular system. Although, this limits the number of modules to about five or six.

Dial Plates

I like how these dial plates turned out. I am using the set-screw chicken-head knobs for pots that have solid non-splined shafts, and press-on knobs for pots that have the splined shafts. I do like to know what number I am on. Unfortunately set-screws knobs for solid shafts that also have numbers are rare.

The plates for the rotary switches have 12 positions. The plates for the pots go from 0 to 10.

In the image above, the white chicken-head is for selecting from three different capacitors for the main (treble) tone control, and the beige knob adjusts the filtering amount.
